title: Stop-loss order clusters make trends unusually rapid past trigger levels (Osler)
claim: Price trends accelerate abnormally when the rate reaches levels where stop-loss orders cluster (e.g. just beyond round numbers / technical levels); the response to stop-loss triggering is larger and lasts longer than the response to take-profit orders, producing self-reinforcing cascades in the breakout direction.

source.label: Osler, C.L. (2005), 'Stop-loss orders and price cascades in currency markets', Journal of International Money and Finance 24(2), 219-241
source.quote: Exchange rate trends are unusually rapid when rates reach exchange rate levels at which stop-loss order have been documented to cluster.

rule_formal: Conditional on price crossing a level L where stop orders cluster (documented: round numbers; ends of recent ranges), E[|r_{t,t+h}| in crossing direction] is elevated vs unconditional; response(stop-loss crossing) > response(take-profit level touch) in size and duration.

numbers.market: Major currency pairs (high-frequency exchange rates); order data from a large FX dealer
numbers.caveats: Abstract gives direction and ordering of effects, not magnitudes; effects documented at intraday horizons (hours), not days

mechanism: Stop-loss orders are positive-feedback trades: crossing the level forces dealers to buy into a rising market / sell into a falling one; clustered stops at obvious levels (range extremes, round numbers) create a queue of forced same-direction flow — this is who is 'forced to buy' after a genuine range break
relevance: Q1: gives the causal mechanism separating follow-through from false break — follow-through requires a populated stop cluster beyond the range edge; implies measurable proxies: proximity of Asian range edge to round numbers (e.g. $5/$10 gold grid), and speed of traverse immediately after the break (cascade signature = unusually rapid move in first minutes)

test_design.lookahead_risk: Round-number distance known at entry; post-break traverse speed measurable in first N minutes after signal — usable realtime only if entry is delayed or managed after trigger

overlap_risk: Post-break speed correlates with day volatility (dead rv variable) at day level, but is measured at event level (minutes around the break), which none of the five dead day-level variables capture; round-number proximity is orthogonal to all five
